subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 7! In this unit you will explore fractions. A fraction is a way of talking about a part of a whole. You meet fractions every single day, even before you learn their names. When Amina cuts a mango in two and shares it with her brother, each piece is one-half. When Hassan folds a piece of anjero into four equal pieces at iftar, each piece is one-quarter. When Khadija pours out a cup that is three-quarters full of camel milk, she is using fractions too.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 3 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: This unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, draw every shape yourself on paper, and only move on when you feel ready. Your AI tutor is available day and night to draw you a new fraction picture, explain a step in a different way, or check your answers. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels tricky.

Why do fractions matter so much? They are everywhere in real life. Traders at the Hargeisa market sell half a kilo of rice or a quarter of a watermelon. Cooks measure three-quarters of a cup of flour when making bariis. Families share food fairly at Ramadan by cutting it into equal parts. Even telling the time uses fractions – we say quarter past and half past the hour. When you become confident with fractions, you are gaining a skill you will use for the rest of your life, and you are building a strong foundation for the harder fraction work, decimals and division that come in later years.

As you work, keep a pencil, a ruler and some paper beside you. Mathematics is not a subject you can learn only by reading – you must do it. Fold real paper, draw the circles and rectangles, shade the parts with a coloured pencil, and say the fraction names out loud. If you get something wrong, that is not a failure; it is simply a sign of exactly what to practise next. Every step you take here makes the next one easier.
Understand that a fraction shows an equal part of a whole.
Name and write the numerator and denominator of a fraction and say what each one means.
Identify and name fractions of shapes – halves, thirds, quarters, fifths and more.
Divide circles, rectangles, squares and triangles into equal parts.
Shade a given fraction of a shape correctly.
Compare fractions with the same denominator and with the same numerator.
Recognise and explain equivalent fractions using pictures.

concepts.title: What Is a Fraction?
concepts.explanation: A fraction is a part of a whole. When you take something complete – like a single flat anjero – and split it into equal parts, each part is a fraction of the whole. The word 'equal' is the most important word in the whole unit, so let us say it again: the parts must be equal in size. Imagine Yusuf has one round flatbread. If he cuts it straight down the middle into two parts of exactly the same size, each part is one-half, which we write as 1/2. If instead he cuts it into four equal parts, each part is…
concepts.example: Notice something surprising: when the whole is cut into more pieces, each piece is smaller. One-quarter of the bread is smaller than one-half of the same bread, because four people share it instead of two. This idea…

concepts.title: The Numerator and the Denominator
concepts.explanation: Every fraction is built from two numbers, one written above the other with a line between them. Each number has a special name and a special job. The numerator is the top number. It tells you how many parts you have.
concepts.example: The denominator is the bottom number. It tells you how many equal parts the whole is divided into.

concepts.title: Equal Parts Are Everything
concepts.explanation: For a fraction to be correct, the parts must be equal in size. This is the golden rule of fractions. If the parts are different sizes, we cannot give them a fair fraction name. Picture a round pancake. If Idris cuts it into four parts but one part is huge and the others are tiny, it would not be fair to call each part 'one-quarter'. A quarter must be exactly one of four equal pieces. Only when all four parts are the same size can each honestly be called 1/4.
concepts.example: So, when you look at any divided shape, ask yourself one question first: are the parts equal? Imagine a circle with four lines through it that make four parts of clearly different sizes – the answer is no, these are not…

concepts.title: Fractions of Different Shapes
concepts.explanation: We can find fractions of many different shapes. Each shape is divided in its own natural way. Let us look at the three you will meet most often. A circle is divided like slices of a pizza or a round cake. You draw the dividing lines from the centre out to the edge, and you make sure the pieces meet at equal angles so they are the same size. Cut a circle into two equal parts and each half looks like a half-moon. Cut it into four and each quarter looks like one slice of a pizza cut into four. Cut it into eight and…
concepts.example: A rectangle is the easiest shape to divide, and it can be divided in more than one way. You can draw lines across it to make equal rows, or lines down it to make equal columns, or both together to make a grid of equal…

concepts.title: Shading a Fraction of a Shape
concepts.explanation: Shading a fraction means colouring in exactly the right number of parts. There are just two steps, and the fraction itself tells you both of them. Look at the denominator and divide the shape into that many equal parts.
concepts.example: Look at the numerator and shade that many of the parts.

concepts.title: Comparing Fractions
concepts.explanation: To compare two fractions means to decide which is bigger, which is smaller, or whether they are equal. There are two easy cases you will use most often. When two fractions have the same denominator (the same bottom number), the pieces are all the same size, so you simply compare the numerators. The fraction with the larger numerator is larger, because it has more of those equal pieces. For example, 3/8 is greater than 2/8 because 3 pieces is more than 2 pieces, and each piece is the same size. We write this as 3/8…
concepts.example: When two fractions have the same numerator (the same top number) but different denominators, you must think about the size of the pieces. The fraction with the smaller denominator is larger, because splitting a whole…

methods.title: How to shade a fraction of a shape
methods.example: A circle is divided into 4 equal parts and 1 part is shaded. What fraction is shaded?
steps: Read the denominator and divide the shape into that many equal parts.
Read the numerator and shade that many parts.
Check: the shaded and unshaded parts add up to the whole.

methods.title: How to compare two fractions
methods.example: A rectangle is divided into 8 equal parts and 3 parts are shaded. What fraction is shaded?
steps: Check whether the denominators are the same or the numerators are the same.
Same denominator: compare the numerators (bigger numerator wins).
Same numerator: compare the denominators (smaller denominator wins).
If unsure, draw both fractions and look at the shaded areas.

methods.title: How to find a fraction of a set
methods.example: Show how to shade 2/6 of a square.
steps: Divide the total by the denominator to make equal groups.
Multiply the answer by the numerator to take the parts you want.
Check the how to find a fraction of a set result carefully.

methods.title: Compare fractions with the same denominator
steps: Solution: The denominators are the same (both 8), so compare the numerators.
5 is more than 3, so 5/8 > 3/8.
Five equal pieces beat three equal pieces of the same size.

methods.title: Compare fractions with the same numerator
steps: Solution: The numerators are the same (both 2), so think about the size of the pieces.
Thirds are bigger than fifths, because the whole is split into fewer parts.
So two thirds is more than two fifths: 2/3 > 2/5.

methods.title: Equivalent fractions
steps: Solution: Draw two identical rectangles.
Split the first into 2 parts and shade 1 (that is 1/2).
Split the second into 4 parts and shade 2 (that is 2/4).
The shaded areas are the same size, so yes, 1/2 = 2/4.

workedExamples.title: Name the shaded fraction
workedExamples.prompt: A circle is divided into 4 equal parts and 1 part is shaded. What fraction is shaded?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Count all the equal parts to get the denominator: there are 4, so the denominator is 4. Count the shaded parts to get the numerator: 1 part is coloured, so the numerator is 1. The shaded fraction is 1/4 (one-quarter).

workedExamples.title: Name a trickier shaded fraction
workedExamples.prompt: A rectangle is divided into 8 equal parts and 3 parts are shaded. What fraction is shaded?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: There are 8 equal parts, so the denominator is 8. There are 3 shaded, so the numerator is 3. The shaded fraction is 3/8 (three-eighths). The unshaded part is 5/8, and 3/8 + 5/8 make the whole.

workedExamples.title: Shade a fraction
workedExamples.prompt: Show how to shade 2/6 of a square.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The denominator is 6, so divide the square into 6 equal parts. The numerator is 2, so shade 2 of them. Two-sixths are now coloured. (You might notice 2/6 is the same amount as 1/3 – they are equivalent.)

workedExamples.title: Compare fractions with the same denominator
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The denominators are the same (both 8), so compare the numerators. 5 is more than 3, so 5/8 > 3/8. Five equal pieces beat three equal pieces of the same size.

workedExamples.title: Compare fractions with the same numerator
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The numerators are the same (both 2), so think about the size of the pieces. Thirds are bigger than fifths, because the whole is split into fewer parts. So two thirds is more than two fifths: 2/3 > 2/5.

workedExamples.title: Equivalent fractions
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Draw two identical rectangles. Split the first into 2 parts and shade 1 (that is 1/2). Split the second into 4 parts and shade 2 (that is 2/4). The shaded areas are the same size, so yes, 1/2 = 2/4. They are equivalent fractions.

workedExamples.title: Fraction of a set
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Divide by the denominator: 16 ÷ 4 = 4, so each quarter is 4 squares. Multiply by the numerator: 4 × 3 = 12. So 3/4 of 16 squares is 12 squares.

workedExamples.title: Order fractions from smallest to largest
workedExamples.prompt: Put these in order from smallest to largest: 1/4, 3/4, 2/4.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The denominators are all the same (4), so order by the numerators. From smallest numerator to largest: 1, 2, 3. So the order is 1/4, 2/4, 3/4.

workedExamples.title: Are these pairs equivalent? Answer yes or no
workedExamples.prompt: Are these pairs equivalent? Answer yes or no: a) 1/2 and 2/4 b) 2/6 and 1/3 c) 3/4 and 6/8.
workedExamples.solution: a) yes, b) yes, c) yes.

workedExamples.title: Divide and shade (describe your picture)
workedExamples.prompt: Divide and shade (describe your picture): a) a rectangle into 6 equal parts, shade 4 – what fraction is shaded? b) a circle into 8 equal parts, shade 3 – what fraction is shaded?

workedExamples.title: Find these fractions of a set
workedExamples.prompt: Find these fractions of a set: a) 1/2 of 8 b) 1/4 of 20 c) 2/3 of 9.

workedExamples.title: Practice 12
workedExamples.prompt: Order these fractions from smallest to largest and explain your rule: 3/8, 3/4, 3/6.
workedExamples.solution: 1) Same numerator (3), so smaller denominator is bigger: 3/8, 3/6, 3/4.

activities.title: The Paper-Folding Fraction Kit
activities.materials: several square sheets of paper and a pencil.
steps: Take one square and fold it once down the middle. Open it – you have made 2 equal parts. Label each part 1/2.
Fold another square twice to make 4 equal parts. Label each part 1/4.
Fold a third square to make 8 equal parts if you can. Label each part 1/8.
Line up your folded papers. Which piece is biggest? Which is smallest? Talk about why more folds make smaller pieces.
Keep your folded papers – they are your own fraction kit for the whole unit.

activities.title: Fraction Art Gallery
activities.materials: coloured paper, scissors, glue and a large sheet for the background.
steps: Cut out circles, squares and rectangles from coloured paper.
Fold each shape to divide it into equal parts – halves, thirds, quarters or fifths.
Cut along the fold lines and arrange the fractional pieces into a picture, such as a flower or a boat.
Glue the pieces down and label each fraction you used. Try to use at least five different fractions.

activities.title: The Fair-Share Snack Game
activities.materials: a real snack that can be shared, such as dates, a banana, or a flatbread, and some family members.
steps: Choose how many people will share the snack – that number becomes your denominator.
Divide the snack into that many equal parts as carefully as you can.
Give each person one part and name the fraction they receive.
Now try giving one person two parts. What fraction do they have? What fraction is left for everyone else?
This game shows that sharing fairly is exactly what fractions are for.

activities.title: Equivalent Fraction Strips
activities.materials: paper cut into several long strips of the same length, a ruler and coloured pencils.
steps: Leave one strip whole and label it 1.
Fold a second strip into 2 equal parts and colour one part – this is 1/2.
Fold a third strip into 4 equal parts and colour two parts – this is 2/4.
Lay the strips on top of each other. Do 1/2 and 2/4 cover the same length? You have just proved they are equivalent!
Make more strips for thirds, fifths and sixths, and hunt for other equivalent fractions.

activities.title: Design a Fraction Tile (Investigation)
steps: Draw a large square on grid paper and divide it into equal parts using the grid lines – try thirds, tenths or eighths.
Shade some of the parts to make a pattern you like.
Write the fraction that is shaded and the fraction that is not shaded. They should add up to the whole.
Swap tiles with a partner. Can they find the fraction you shaded? Explain how you know your fraction is correct.

activities.title: Whole-Shape Detective (Family Task)
activities.materials: paper, scissors and a family member.
steps: Cut out a shape and tell your family member it is one part of a bigger whole – for example, 'this triangle is 1/4 of a shape'.
Ask them to draw what the whole shape might have looked like.
Check together that the whole would be made of four equal parts the same size as your piece.
Swap roles and try again with a different fraction, such as 1/3 or 1/5.

After your activities, talk or write about: Which activity helped you understand fractions best? Which was the trickiest part, and why? What did you learn about equal parts and equivalent fractions? What would you like more practice with? Share your answers with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: Key rule 1
rules.text: What a Fraction Is A fraction is an equal part of a whole. Top number = numerator = how many parts you have. Bottom number = denominator = how many equal parts in the whole.

rules.title: Equal Parts Rule
rules.text: The parts must be equal in size to be a fraction. Equal means the same amount of space, even if the shapes look different.

rules.title: Comparing Rule
rules.text: Same denominator: the bigger numerator is the bigger fraction. Same numerator: the smaller denominator is the bigger fraction. The open mouth of > or < always points at the bigger fraction.

terms: An equal part of a whole or a set
Top number – how many parts you have
Bottom number – equal parts in the whole
Parts that are the same size
Different fractions worth the same amount
commonMistakes: Bottom = parts in whole; top = parts you have
Divide by bottom, then multiply by top

realProblems.prompt: A pizza is cut into 8 equal slices. Ibrahim eats 3 slices. What fraction of the pizza has he eaten, and what fraction is left?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: A class of 20 students has 3/5 girls. How many girls are there, and how many boys?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: A ribbon is 24 cm long. Zahra uses 2/3 of it. How many centimetres does she use, and how many are left?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: At the market, Sumaya has 15 mangoes and sells 3/5 of them. How many does she sell?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: A garden is divided into 6 equal sections. Vegetables fill 1/2 of it and flowers fill 1/3 of it. How many sections have vegetables, how many have flowers, and how many are empty?
realProblems.answer: Vegetables 1/2 of 6 = 3 sections; flowers 1/3 of 6 = 2 sections; empty = 6 − 3 − 2 = 1 section.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Vegetables 1/2 of 6 = 3 sections; flowers 1/3 of 6 = 2 sections; empty = 6 − 3 − 2 = 1 section.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Find 3/4 of 16 squares. Show the divide step and the multiply step.
Draw two identical rectangles to prove that 2/3 = 4/6.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: First rectangle split into 3 with 2 shaded; second split into 6 with 4 shaded – the shaded areas match, so 2/3 = 4/6.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Omar shades 2/5 of a strip and says the unshaded part is 4/5. Explain his mistake and give the correct answer.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 2/5 shaded means 3/5 is unshaded, because 2/5 + 3/5 make the whole; 4/5 is wrong.

questions.question: What does “Fraction” mean?
options: An equal part of a whole or a set
Top number – how many parts you have
Bottom number – equal parts in the whole
Parts that are the same size
questions.answer: An equal part of a whole or a set
questions.hint: Use the Unit 7 Math Words & Symbols reference.

selfAssessment: I can this unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, draw every shape yourself on paper, and only move on when you feel ready. Your AI tutor is available day and night to draw you a new fraction picture, explain a step in a different way, or check your answers. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels tricky.

I can why do fractions matter so much? They are everywhere in real life. Traders at the Hargeisa market sell half a kilo of rice or a quarter of a watermelon. Cooks measure three-quarters of a cup of flour when making bariis. Families share food fairly at Ramadan by cutting it into equal parts. Even telling the time uses fractions – we say quarter past and half past the hour. When you become confident with fractions, you are gaining a skill you will use for the rest of your life, and you are building a strong foundation for the harder fraction work, decimals and division that come in later years.

I can as you work, keep a pencil, a ruler and some paper beside you. Mathematics is not a subject you can learn only by reading – you must do it. Fold real paper, draw the circles and rectangles, shade the parts with a coloured pencil, and say the fraction names out loud. If you get something wrong, that is not a failure; it is simply a sign of exactly what to practise next. Every step you take here makes the next one easier.
I can understand that a fraction shows an equal part of a whole.
I can name and write the numerator and denominator of a fraction and say what each one means.
I can identify and name fractions of shapes – halves, thirds, quarters, fifths and more.
I can divide circles, rectangles, squares and triangles into equal parts.
I can shade a given fraction of a shape correctly.
